title: In the End, It Is Jayalalithaa Who Will Be Deceived!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. Two passages in the OCR-derived Tamil were severely damaged. The source scans establish that Kalaignar described prohibition as a temporary postponement forced by the State's financial condition, and that the DMK Government restored prohibition in 1974 after reopening toddy and arrack shops in 1971. The Tamil reading copy below uses those direct source transcriptions and repairs only other unambiguous OCR defects. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Chief Minister Jayalalithaa began her Legislative Assembly election campaign on 9 April 2016. A vast stage was erected, with the lady alone seated high upon a separate dais. Three or four armed police officers stood guard, while all the candidates remained below the stage, deferentially standing with folded hands. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa then completed the recital of her characteristic “epic of abuse.” Although she does not answer the opposition's continuing allegations, we respect democracy and are duty-bound—and know how—to answer the allegations she has made.

While she was in office, social guardians such as Sasi Perumal lost their lives over the issue of prohibition, yet Jayalalithaa did not come forward to announce that prohibition would be introduced. She filed sedition cases against people such as Kovan for singing campaign songs about prohibition. Now she has announced that, if she returns to power, prohibition will be introduced in stages. Whether the people of Tamil Nadu are prepared to believe her is another question.

In her speech, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said that neither I nor the DMK had any right to speak about prohibition and repeated the same worn-out claim she has made for years: that prohibition in Tamil Nadu was repealed under a DMK Government. We have answered that allegation many times. Yet because she has found nothing else to say or criticise, she has repeated it once more.
One can wake those who are asleep; can one wake those who pretend to sleep?

Although prohibition was postponed under the DMK Government, I stated even at the time that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam had not taken the decision wholeheartedly. I repeatedly explained that Tamil Nadu's financial condition then was the reason.
What did I say when I addressed the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly while postponing prohibition?

> “Gandhiji said that this policy, born of a sacred purpose, should be extended across the whole of India. Yet not only were the State Chief Ministers—his commanders who stood arrayed beneath the shadow of the flag he carried—unable to follow that policy; it was also disregarded by the Mahatma's successors who governed at the Centre. That is a painful truth. How long can Tamil Nadu protect itself like a piece of camphor left unlit within a blazing ring of fire?”

Jayalalithaa cannot conceal that I said this in the Legislative Assembly. I announced the postponement of the Prohibition Act only because conscience left us no other course. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa conveniently omitted all this from her speech and, at the very mention of an election, has set out to deliver falsehoods.
On 29 June 1971, replying to the general discussion on the financial statement, I said:

> “I respect and appreciate the feelings of the honourable members and friends from allied parties. Some of them have even pleaded with me. They said they were imploring me. I am younger than all of them. Please take back that pleading and imploring. We have come to this decision because there is no other way. Even then, we have said only that we are postponing it.”
That is how I explained the position with great humility and affection.

In her speech, Jayalalithaa exaggerated and spun an imaginary tale that statesman Rajaji came to my home in pouring rain and pleaded with me as though falling at my feet. It was not raining when Rajaji met me about prohibition. Nor did he fall at my feet and plead. It is true that he met me and made the request; it is equally true that I explained the Government's financial position to him.

Although the DMK Government opened toddy and arrack shops in 1971, it was under the DMK Government itself, in 1974, that liquor shops were closed again and prohibition restored. Those who repeatedly speak of the opening while conveniently covering up the closure should consider whether that is fair. When recounting history, one must say what happened exactly as it happened; one must not impose a distorted argument for personal reasons.

Does not postponement itself mean that a policy will later return to operation? Was legislation implementing complete prohibition in Tamil Nadu not passed unanimously by the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 22 August 1974, during the DMK Government itself? Why did Chief Minister Jayalalithaa omit this completely from her speech? As usual, has she not knowingly spoken a falsehood on this issue too?

What did the AIADMK Government that followed do? The suspension of prohibition introduced under the DMK Government was itself withdrawn under the DMK Government, and prohibition returned to operation. That is the truth, the truth. Can anyone call it untrue?
But in 1981, did the AIADMK Government repeal prohibition once again to permit the sale of toddy and arrack, or did it not? Can anyone establish that it did not?

When Jayalalithaa had to step down for a short period because of a Supreme Court judgment and O. Panneerselvam was Chief Minister, was it not announced that foreign liquor varieties would go on sale from the first week of January 2002 at the cheap price of fifteen rupees per 100 millilitres?

In 2003, was it not the AIADMK Government led by Jayalalithaa that created TASMAC for liquor sales and opened government liquor shops from town to town? Were TASMAC shops not opened everywhere during Jayalalithaa's rule? Did this happen or not?

A few months ago, I announced that, if the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam returned to office in Tamil Nadu, steps would be taken to implement prohibition. Leaders and frontline members of various parties, social activists, journalists and women's organisations contacted me by telephone and in person, welcomed the announcement and called it a good decision. It is no exaggeration to say that my announcement created a profound impact across every section.

On 22 December 2008, PMK leader Dr Ramadoss and leaders of several organisations met me and urged the implementation of prohibition in Tamil Nadu. I reminded them that the DMK Government, which shared their view, had closed 1,300 bars across Tamil Nadu after coming to power in 2006 and had similarly closed 128 retail liquor shops.

I also announced that continuing efforts would be made to achieve complete prohibition in stages. Until then, the Government had decided to act carefully to prevent liquor shops from being located near public places such as temples, schools, colleges, mosques and churches. Did we not announce that the operating hours of liquor shops would be reduced by one hour, and issue the corresponding order?

Yesterday Jayalalithaa spoke as though prohibition had first been postponed in Tamil Nadu under the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam Government. That too is untrue; it is another distortion of history by Jayalalithaa. When a Madras Presidency Government was formed under Rajaji in 1937, he implemented prohibition, which until then had not existed in the Presidency. But even that ended in 1939. In 1937, while Rajaji was Chief Minister, prohibition existed in only four of the Madras Presidency's twenty-five districts: Salem, Chittoor, Cuddapah and North Arcot. The pre-1937 position continued in the other districts.

Complete prohibition was implemented in Tamil Nadu only after Omandurar became Chief Minister in 1948. At all other times—before 1937 and from 1939 until 1948—prohibition was not in force in Tamil Nadu. A moment's reflection on this history shows how perverse Jayalalithaa's argument is that the DMK Government was the first to introduce liquor into Tamil Nadu, and what a debased form of politics it is to hold only the DMK and me responsible.
Further, my dear friend M.G.R. issued a statement on prohibition on 13 September 1969. At its conclusion he said:

> “On the policy of prohibition, I can state more firmly and boldly than others that DMK leader and Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Kalaignar has greater concern and commitment to the belief that this policy is necessary for the people than any individual or party not only in Tamil Nadu but anywhere on the Indian subcontinent.”
Did Jayalalithaa forget—or conceal—that he later wrote about this at length in his extended *Ananda Vikatan* series titled “Why Was I Born?”

Gandhian Sasi Perumal had not even reached the age of sixty. His protest was to demand the closure of one TASMAC shop in Kanniyakumari district. Had the Government agreed to close that single shop, the problem would never have arisen, nor would protests have spread throughout Tamil Nadu. But what did this Government ever pay attention to, that it should attend to this? How many hours could he remain atop a mobile-phone tower? Had the responsible officials arrived within an hour, discussed the issue and reached a decision, would Sasi Perumal—who had dedicated his life to prohibition—have met an untimely death?
A few days ago, *Ananda Vikatan* wrote in an editorial titled “Let Evil Depart”:

> “Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has so far said not a single word about prohibition, reducing the number of liquor shops, or the protests against TASMAC outlets. Protests against TASMAC had begun across Tamil Nadu in full force even before Sasi Perumal's death. They intensified afterward. College students, homemakers and public-welfare organisations continue to protest throughout Tamil Nadu. Every political party has entered the field in its own way. > > “As the protests intensified, reports were leaked from government circles to the media that the Chief Minister would announce on 15 August a reduction in the number of liquor shops. The Chief Minister, who had not spoken a single word about the protests, issued a statement warning that stern action would be taken against those engaging in violence at TASMAC shops. The Chief Minister must announce her Government's position on the liquor trade conducted by the State itself. If the Government deliberately delays, thinking that announcements concerning TASMAC near the election may help it harvest votes, that would be gravely wrong. Once something is known to be harmful, it is right to abandon it that very moment. Let evil depart at once... let good begin at once.”
That is how *Ananda Vikatan* developed its editorial argument.

Jayalalithaa has now acted in a manner that confirms the editorial. Just as she continually employs deception in everything, she has announced that prohibition will be introduced in stages if the AIADMK returns to power. She does so with the fraudulent hope that she can deceive the people during the election and harvest their votes. They say they will do after returning to power what they had no desire to do during their five years in office, and thus deceive the whole town. The people will remember M.G.R.'s song: “Is it right to make a living by deceiving others?”

In January this year, during the debate on the Governor's Address in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, Minister Natham Viswanathan said: “We say that we will support prohibition if the Central Government introduces it throughout India.” When DMK Legislature Party Leader M.K. Stalin asked with deep concern that prohibition be implemented at least in stages, following the neighbouring States, Minister Natham Viswanathan rejected even that request.

But now that an election is approaching, even though the Central Government has not introduced prohibition throughout India, Jayalalithaa says that they will introduce it gradually if they return to power. Is this not merely an attempt to deceive the whole town? She may deceive anyone she wishes with such words, but she cannot deceive the Tamil people. She has taken up diversion and historical distortion in her election campaign; in the end, it is Jayalalithaa who will be deceived!
With affection,
M.K.
